{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Key Findings

# In Southern Nigeria, states in all zones are also impacted by increasing SAM admissions. South South's increases are larger than expected historical trends; however, improvement is seen by August 2025.

- In South South, 5 states (Akwa-Ibom, Bayelsa, Cross Rivers, Delta, Rivers) saw an increase in SAM admissions, In all but Bayelsa, these increases were greater than predicted by historical trends, suggesting possible worsening nutrition status. However, all states saw a decrease in SAM in August 2025, indicating a possibly improving situation.

- In South East, 4 states (Abia, Anambra, Ebonyi, Enugu) had some observed increases in SAM admission in 2025, but findings from different analyses conflicted or were inconsistent, leading to limited conclusions.

- In South West, only 2 states (Ogun, Ondo) had observed increases in SAM admittance; however, these increases were in line with historical trends."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"In Southern zones, 11 states saw an increase in SAM admissions from April to July '25; however spikes were less acute and largely recovered by August '25"}}

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Key Findings
- In Northern Nigeria, states in all zones are experiencing increasing SAM admissions for children under 5, particularly in April and June - July 2025.
- North Central and North East are also seeing more SAM admissions than expected based on historical trends.
- While many North Central states have a worsening trendline, the absolute number of children admitted for SAM is still relatively low (200 - 600 children a month in impacted states).
- Comparatively, impacted states in North West and North East are seeing much higher admissions in absolute terms (e.g., 15,000 children in Zamfara), suggesting that targeted efforts in affected North East and North West states will have the greatest impact.
- Increasing diarrhea cases are reported in a sub-set of these states. All states in North East are reporting a greater-than-expected number of cases and only two (Gombe, Yobe) are showing signs of a recovery by August 2025."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Executive Summary: 23 states reported increases in SAM admission between April and July 2025, & 15 states have reported increases in diarrhea cases in children under 5"}}

- The presence of extremely high values (outliers)
- The percentage of facility-months with complete data for the selected indicators (indicator completeness)

{"type":"freeform","cover":{"titleText":"","titleTextRelFontSize":10,"subTitleText":"","subTitleTextRelFontSize":6,"presenterText":"","presenterTextRelFontSize":4,"dateText":"","dateTextRelFontSize":3,"logos":[]},"section":{"sectionText":"","sectionTextRelFontSize":8,"smallerSectionText":"","smallerSectionTextRelFontSize":5},"freeform":{"content":[[{"type":"text","stretch":true,"fillArea":false,"textSize":1,"textBackground":"grey","placeholderInvisible":false,"placeholderStretch":true,"placeholderHeight":50,"useFigureAdditionalScale":false,"figureAdditionalScale":1,"imgHeight":300,"imgFit":"inside","imgStretch":true,"hideFigureCaption":false,"hideFigureSubCaption":false,"hideFigureFootnote":false,"markdown":"# Limitations
- This analysis is focused on trends, with each state's current performance compared to its own trends for SAM admission. States with increasing trends have been identified for further investigation. Absolute values of SAM admissions across states have not been compared in this analysis.
- Nigeria's DHIS-2 does not have data recorded for "children <5 years treated for SAM" from May to August 2025. This indicator was therefore excluded from the analysis.
- August 2025 data was extracted from DHIS-2 on September 29, 2025. While the majority of August data was likely reported and logged into DHIS-2 at that time, August 2025 totals may still be underreported. Updating this analysis monthly may result in increases in the previous last month's data totals due to late data submissions.
- This data only captures reported SAM admissions for children under 5. It will not capture (1) children with SAM who do not present to health facilities and (2) children with SAM who present but are not admitted, and (3) children with SAM who present and are admitted to health facilities that do not report to DHIS-2 (mainly higher level hospitals and private facilities).
- Similar limitations are present for the number of new diarrhea cases for children under 5. This analysis only captures cases that were reported in DHIS-2 and therefore does not include community cases that did not present to a reporting facility.

# Recommendations
- Follow up should be conducted to states demonstrating an increase in SAM admissions to triangulate this analysis with other data sources and to validate these findings.
- Statistical models may need further adjustments to account for seasonality which appears in the SAM admissions dataset."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"Recommendations and Limitations"}}

# In Northern Nigeria, states in all zones are experiencing increasing SAM admissions for children under 5. North Central and North East seeing more SAM admissions than expected based on historical trends, with no sign of decline or improvement in North Central.

- In North Central, 5 states (Benue, FCT, Kogi, Nasarawa, and Niger) are showing an increase in SAMs admission from April to August 2025. All five state increased are larger-than-expected based on historical trends, suggesting a worsening nutrition situation, with Kogi and Nasarawa showing no signs of recovery in August 2025.

- In North East, 4 states (Adamawa, Bauchi, Gombe, and Taraba) have increased SAM admissions from April to August 2025, with all states but Taraba seeing declines by August 2025. Only 1 state (Adamawa) is seeing increases above expected historical trends. This suggests that North East could be experiencing relatively typical seasonal fluctuations in SAM admissions.

- In North West, 3 states (Jigawa, Katsina, and Zamfara) experienced increased SAM admissions between April and July 2025. Only Zamfara saw greater than expected SAM admissions based on historical trends and is experiencing continued elevation in SAM admission by August 2025. This suggests that North West could be experiencing relatively typical seasonal fluctuations in SAM admissions."}]],"useHeader":true,"headerText":"In Northern Nigeria, 12 states experienced spikes in SAM admission from April to July 25, with 8 states experiencing a SAM admissions decline by August '25 and 4 states having no sign of recovery"}}
